event: English navigator John Davis, aboard the Desire, is often credited with the first sighting of the Falkland Islands.
English commander Richard Hawkins sights the islands, naming them Hawkins' Maidenland.
Dutch navigator Sebald de Weert makes the first widely acknowledged sighting, leading to the islands being called the Sebald Islands on early charts.
English captain John Strong makes the first recorded landing and names the sound between the two main islands after Viscount Falkland, a name later applied to the whole archipelago.
French explorer Louis Antoine de Bougainville founds the first settlement, Port Louis, on East Falkland and claims the islands for France.
British captain John Byron lands at Saunders Island, claims the islands for Britain, and the following year a settlement named Port Egmont is established.
France cedes its settlement to Spain, which renames it Puerto Soledad and governs it from Buenos Aires.
A Spanish expedition expels the British from Port Egmont, bringing Britain and Spain to the brink of war.
A diplomatic agreement allows the British to return to Port Egmont, with the question of sovereignty left unresolved.
Britain withdraws its garrison from Port Egmont for economic reasons, leaving a plaque asserting continued claim to the islands.
Spain withdraws its garrison from Puerto Soledad amid the Latin American wars of independence, leaving the islands without a permanent population.
The United Provinces of the Río de la Plata (Argentina) proclaim sovereignty over the islands through Colonel David Jewett at Puerto Soledad.
Merchant Luis Vernet establishes a colony at Puerto Soledad under Buenos Aires authorization, developing fishing, sealing, and cattle ventures.
The American warship USS Lexington raids Puerto Soledad in retaliation for Vernet's seizure of US sealing vessels, devastating the settlement.
Buenos Aires sends a military commander to the islands, but a mutiny results in his murder.
British forces reassert sovereignty, expelling the Argentine garrison and beginning continuous British control of the islands.
Charles Darwin visits the islands aboard HMS Beagle, recording observations of their geology and wildlife.
Britain decides to establish a permanent colony, and the following year a lieutenant governor is appointed.
Stanley is established as the new capital, replacing Port Louis owing to its superior harbour.
The Falkland Islands Company is granted a royal charter, becoming the dominant force in the islands' wool-based economy.
Sheep farming overtakes wild cattle as the mainstay of the economy, establishing the wool trade that would define the islands for over a century.
Britain formally asserts sovereignty over the Falkland Islands Dependencies, including South Georgia and other southern territories.
The Battle of the Falkland Islands sees a British naval squadron destroy a German fleet off the coast during the First World War.
Stanley serves as a base supporting the pursuit of the German cruiser Admiral Graf Spee early in the Second World War.
The UN General Assembly adopts Resolution 2065, inviting Britain and Argentina to negotiate over the sovereignty dispute.
A communications agreement establishes air and sea links between the islands and the Argentine mainland.
Argentina invades the islands on 2 April, prompting the Falklands War; Britain retakes them after intense fighting, with Argentine surrender on 14 June.
Falkland Islanders are granted full British citizenship under the British Nationality (Falkland Islands) Act.
A new constitution grants the islands greater internal self-government, and South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands become a separate territory.
The islands declare a fisheries conservation and management zone, transforming the economy through the sale of squid fishing licences.
Britain and Argentina restore full diplomatic relations, broken since the 1982 war.
Oil exploration drilling begins in waters around the islands, signalling potential hydrocarbon wealth.
An agreement restores air links and eases restrictions on Argentine citizens visiting the islands.
A new constitution comes into force, strengthening self-governance and enshrining the islanders' right to self-determination.
Significant oil discoveries are announced in the North Falkland Basin, notably the Sea Lion field.
A referendum sees Falkland Islanders vote overwhelmingly to remain a British Overseas Territory, with 99.8 percent in favour.
A new permanent cemetery and memorial work continues as Argentina and Britain cooperate to identify war dead buried at Darwin.
The islands close their borders and impose measures in response to the global COVID-19 pandemic, with tourism severely disrupted.
